## High-Level Review Feedback

This paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by formalizing the over-squashing phenomenon in graph neural networks through the lens of discrete curvature, and the introduction of Balanced Forman curvature as a tractable lower bound for Ollivier curvature is a clever and practically useful innovation. The paper is generally well-structured and the writing is clear, with a logical progression from problem definition to theoretical analysis to algorithmic solution and experimental validation. However, there are several significant issues that temper the contribution. First, the paper claims to be "the first theoretical study of the bottleneck and over-squashing phenomena...from a geometric perspective," but this framing somewhat overstates the novelty given existing work like Alon & Yahav (2021) that already identified bottlenecks and proposed rewiring—the paper would benefit from a more precise articulation of how its geometric approach yields *distinct* insights beyond heuristic observations. Second, while the theoretical analysis linking negative curvature to over-squashing is elegant, the connection between the Jacobian-based sensitivity measure and the curvature-based rewiring remains somewhat circumstantial; the paper would be strengthened by a formal theorem proving that reducing negative curvature provably increases the Jacobian-based information propagation metric. Third, the experimental evaluation is limited in scope and raises concerns about practical applicability: the proposed Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow is tested on relatively small, standard benchmarks, and it is unclear how the method scales to large graphs or whether the curvature computation becomes a bottleneck itself. Additionally, the empirical comparisons against diffusion-based rewiring are informative but would benefit from a more systematic ablation study isolating the effect of curvature-based rewiring from other architectural choices. Finally, the paper's title and framing suggest a comprehensive understanding of over-squashing, but the analysis focuses exclusively on graph structure without considering how different message functions or aggregation schemes interact with curvature to amplify or mitigate squashing—this omission limits the generality of the claimed "understanding."

comments.title: Missing right parenthesis after S_1(j) in definition of ♯_□^[i](i,j)
comments.explanation: The notation "k = j" inside the set builder seems garbled. It should likely be "k ≠ j" (i.e., k not equal to j) to exclude the target node j itself when listing neighbors of i that form a 4-cycle with j. The OCR may have introduced a spurious equals sign. Since this is a definitional issue that could affect a reader's understanding of the 4-cycle condition, it is worth flagging.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing in Q_j definition (Theorem 4)
comments.quote: Then there exists Q_j ⊂ S_2(i) satisfying |Q_j| > δ^{-1}
comments.explanation: The variable δ is defined in condition (ii) as δ = -Ric(i,j) - (1/d_i + 1/d_j + γ_max(i,j)/2). However, it is not immediately clear that this δ is positive (as would be needed for |Q_j| > δ^{-1} to be meaningful). Under condition (ii), Ric(i,j) is sufficiently negative, but the expression for δ involves degrees and γ_max, so its sign depends on the actual values. The authors do not provide an explicit guarantee that δ > 0 for all negatively curved edges satisfying (ii). If δ were negative, the inequality |Q_j| > δ^{-1} would be trivial or ill-defined. A brief note in the text or a small derivation would resolve this.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of S_1(i) and S_2(i) not fully specified in Theorem 4
comments.quote: Then there exists Q_j ⊂ S_2(i) satisfying |Q_j| > δ^{-1} and for 0 ≤ ℓ_0 ≤ L-2 we have ...
comments.explanation: The notation S_2(i) appears without a formal definition in the provided excerpt. Earlier in Section 2.1, the paper defines S_r(i) as the sphere of radius r (set of nodes at distance exactly r from i). This is standard, but the reader might momentarily question whether S_2(i) includes i itself or only distance-2 nodes. The context makes it clear that Q_j is a subset of nodes at distance 2 from i, so this is not a major issue, but explicitly restating the definition in Theorem 4 would improve clarity.

comments.title: Condition (ii) in Theorem 4 uses Undefined γ_max(i,j) with no explicit bound
comments.quote: γ_max(i,j) ≤ 1/4 d_i^2
comments.explanation: The quantity γ_max(i,j) is defined earlier as 'the maximal number of 4-cycles based at i∼j traversing a common node'. However, the bound γ_max(i,j) ≤ 1/4 d_i^2 is not derived or justified in the text. While this bound may follow from combinatorial reasoning (e.g., each common node can be involved in at most (d_i choose something) cycles), the authors do not provide a proof or reference. Since this bound is used to ensure that the curvature condition (ii) is well-behaved, a brief justification would strengthen the argument.

comments.title: Overclaim in abstract regarding 'first theoretical study'
comments.quote: This paper, to our knowledge, is the first theoretical study of the bottleneck and over-squashing phenomena in message passing neural networks from a geometric perspective.
comments.explanation: While the paper is indeed a pioneering geometric analysis, the phrase 'first theoretical study' is slightly overbroad. Prior work (Alon & Yahav, 2021) already provided theoretical discussion (though not geometric) of bottlenecks and over-squashing. The authors qualify this with 'from a geometric perspective', which is more accurate, but the phrasing in the abstract could be read as claiming the first theoretical study overall. This is a minor framing issue that does not affect the paper's contributions.

comments.title: Misapplied indexing in Lemma 1's statement: 's ∈ S_{r+1}(i)' unclear
comments.quote: Let i, s ∈ V with s ∈ S_{r+1}(i) . If |∇φ_ℓ| ≤ α and |∇ψ_ℓ| ≤ β for 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ r, then
comments.explanation: The notation S_{r+1}(i) is not defined in the passage. The authors previously defined S_r(i) (the sphere of radius r) but used subscript 1 for one-hop neighbors earlier (S_1(i)), so S_{r+1}(i) is presumably the set of nodes at distance exactly r+1 from i. However, the lemma is supposed to bound the Jacobian ∂h_i^{(r)}/∂x_s, where s is at distance r+1. The use of S_{r+1}(i) is consistent with the context (the example mentions d_G(i,s)=r+1), so it is clear what is intended. This is a minor ambiguity in notation that a careful reader can resolve, but it would be better to formally define S_r(i) earlier in the passage (it is only used implicitly). Not a real error.

comments.title: Missing exponent in Lemma 1: bound likely involves (Â)^{r+1} but text shows Â^{r+1}
comments.explanation: The OCR has introduced formatting errors: the exponent in '(Aˆ [r] +1)' should be read as (Â)^{r+1} (the (r+1)-th power of the normalized augmented adjacency matrix). Similarly '2 − 1 3 − r' is meant to be '2^{-1} 3^{-r}'. The example assumes a binary tree where each non-root node has degree 3 inside the receptive field, leading to (Â)^{r+1}_{is} = (1/2) * (1/3)^r. The intended meaning is clear from the context and standard GNN analysis. No real issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent index in Theorem 4: L is depth, but condition uses ℓ_0 ≤ L-2
comments.quote: Consider a MPNN as in equation 1. Let i ∼ j with d_i ≤ d_j and assume that: (i) |∇φ_ℓ| ≤ α and |∇ψ_ℓ| ≤ β for each 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ L − 1, with L ≥ 2 the depth of the MPNN. Then there exists Q_j ⊂ S_2(i) satisfying |Q_j| > δ^{−1} and for 0 ≤ ℓ_0 ≤ L − 2 we have
comments.explanation: The theorem states L ≥ 2, but then requires ℓ_0 ≤ L-2. For L=2, this forces ℓ_0=0 only, which is fine. However, the result is supposed to show that negatively curved edges cause over-squashing over two layers from node i to nodes at distance 2 (since Q_j ⊂ S_2(i)). The bound involves the Jacobian computed at layer ℓ_0+2. The condition ℓ_0 ≤ L-2 ensures that the MPNN has at least ℓ_0+2 layers to propagate. This is logically consistent: for L=2, ℓ_0=0 is the only allowed value. The text could be clearer about why the bound depends on the depth, but it is not an error.

comments.title: Potential sign error in Proposition 5: inequality direction for Cheeger constant
comments.quote: If Ric(i, j) ≥ k > 0 for all i ∼ j, then λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2.
comments.explanation: The Cheeger inequality states λ_1/2 ≤ h_G ≤ √(2λ_1) (for the normalized Laplacian). The statement here says λ_1/2 ≥ h_G, which would be the reverse of the lower bound. However, the authors reference Lin et al. (2011) and Theorem 2 to derive this. The claim is that positive curvature gives a lower bound on the spectral gap (λ_1), and then via Cheeger inequality, h_G is bounded below by k/2. The inequality direction as written 'λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2' would imply λ_1 ≥ 2h_G and h_G ≥ k/2. This is plausible if λ_1 is large enough. But the standard Cheeger inequality for the normalized Laplacian is h_G ≥ λ_1/2 (the 'easy' direction). So the statement λ_1/2 ≥ h_G is the opposite of the usual inequality. Unless the paper uses a different normalization or defines λ_1 differently (e.g., the largest eigenvalue of some other matrix), this appears to be a sign error. Given that the next section discusses diffusion-based rewiring and Cheeger constant, this could mislead a reader about the relationship between curvature and bottleneck. This is a moderate issue because it may indicate a misunderstanding of the Cheeger inequality direction or a nonstandard convention.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of softmax sampling in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Sample index k, l with probability softmax(τ x)_kl and add edge k ∼ l to G.
comments.explanation: The description says 'Calculate vector x where x_{kl} = Ric_{kl}(i,j) − Ric(i,j), the improvement to Ric(i,j) from adding edge k ∼ l where k ∈ B_1(i), l ∈ B_1(j)'. It then says 'Sample index k, l with probability softmax(τ x)_kl'. The softmax function is typically applied to a vector, but x here is described as having entries indexed by pairs (k,l). It is unclear whether x is flattened into a vector or if a 2D softmax (over both indices) is intended. Additionally, 'Ric_{kl}(i,j)' is not defined; the notation suggests it might be the curvature of edge (i,j) after adding edge (k,l), but this is not explained. The algorithm is underspecified: a reader cannot reproduce how to compute the improvement x_{kl} without knowing how Recomputing curvature after each edge addition is handled. This makes the method difficult to implement as described.

comments.title: Contradiction in curvature sign convention for tree example
comments.quote: In a discrete hyperbolic geometry (Figure 2c), the mutual distance of the edge endpoints would have grown compared to that of i and j (tree).
comments.explanation: This prose correctly associates trees with negative curvature (hyperbolic geometry). However, earlier Figure 2 caption labels (c) as 'Tree (<0)', which is consistent. The confusion arises later when discussing Figure 3 and the calculation of ♯_□: the authors say 'both without 4,6 because of the triangle 1-6-0'. This is fine. The potential issue is that the curvature definition (Definition 1) is not fully displayed; only a partial formula is shown. The passage says 'The curvature is negative when i ∼ j behaves as a bridge between S_1(i) and S_1(j)'. This is consistent with the tree example. No actual error here, but the partial formula for Ric(i,j) in the OCR (with missing terms) makes it impossible to verify the curvature values in Table 1. For instance, Table 1 says for a tree T_r, Ric = 4/(r+1) − 2, which is negative for r ≥ 2. The reader cannot verify this from the truncated formula. This is more of an OCR truncation issue than an author error, but it affects understanding.

comments.title: Incorrect inequality in Proposition 5 (Cheeger constant bound)
comments.quote: **Proposition 5.** _If_ Ric( _i, j_ ) _≥ k >_ 0 _for any edge i ∼ j, then λ_ 1 _/_ 2 _≥ hG ≥[k]_ 2 _._
comments.explanation: The Cheeger inequality for the normalized Laplacian (Chung & Graham, 1997) states λ₁ ≤ 2h_G, i.e., h_G ≥ λ₁/2. The proposition claims the opposite inequality λ₁/2 ≥ h_G, which is generally false. For example, a complete graph K_n with large n has λ₁ = n/(n-1) ≈ 1 and h_G ≈ 1 (since the minimum cut is about half the edges), giving λ₁/2 ≈ 0.5 < 1 = h_G. This contradicts the stated bound. The intended inequality is likely h_G ≥ λ₁/2, which together with h_G ≥ k/2 would be correct under the given curvature condition. The reversal appears to be a sign error. This affects the paper's claim about controlling the spectral gap via curvature, and could mislead readers about the relation between curvature and bottleneckedness.

comments.title: Inconsistency in claim about SDRF adding/removing similar numbers of edges
comments.quote: In comparison, SDRF adds and removes a similar number of edges and approximately preserves the degree distribution.
comments.explanation: Table 3 shows that for several datasets, the percentage of edges removed by SDRF is substantially larger than the percentage added. For example, Cornell: +7.8% vs -33.3%; Texas: +2.4% vs -10.4%; Wisconsin: +1.4% vs -7.5%; Actor: +5.4% vs -9.9%. Only about half the datasets (Chameleon, Squirrel, Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) have roughly equal percentages. The statement that SDRF 'adds and removes a similar number of edges' is not universally true and could mislead a reader about the extent of edge removals relative to additions.

comments.title: Theorem 4 conclusion does not match the bound derived in the statement
comments.quote: Then there exists Qj ⊂ S_2(i) satisfying |Qj| > δ^{-1} and for 0 ≤ ℓ_0 ≤ L − 2 we have ... the Jacobian of h_k^{(ℓ_0+2)} w.r.t. x_i is bounded (over-squashing occurs). Negatively curved edges cause bottlenecks.
comments.explanation: The passage states that Theorem 4 proves that 'negatively curved edges are those causing the graph bottleneck and thus leading to the over-squashing phenomenon.' However, the theorem's conclusion as presented appears to claim both that a large set Q_j exists and that the Jacobian is bounded. The notion of 'bounded' here is unclear in context: if the Jacobian is bounded for all nodes in Q_j, that would mean the influence is limited, which is the over-squashing claim. But the inequality involves Ric-like quantities multiplied by powers of the normalized adjacency matrix. Without seeing the full expression in the paper (only the first line is provided in the quote), it is ambiguous whether the bound is a small number (confirming over-squashing) or a trivial bound. The summary says 'the Jacobian ... is bounded (over-squashing occurs)', but a bounded Jacobian could be either large or small. The reader cannot verify that the bound indeed implies a non-trivial restriction of information flow. The authors may have intended to show that the Jacobian is bounded from above by a quantity that is small due to negative curvature, but the excerpt alone does not make this clear.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for Balanced Forman curvature in Definition 1 vs. table
comments.quote: We refer to Ric as Balanced Forman curvature. ... Table 1: Examples of the Balanced Forman curvature. ... Complete K_n: n/(n-1)
comments.explanation: Definition 1 defines the curvature as Ric(i,j), and the prose says 'We refer to Ric as Balanced Forman curvature'. However, in Table 1, the row for the complete graph K_n shows a value of n/(n-1). According to Definition 1, for the complete graph K_n, each edge i∼j has d_i = d_j = n-1, ♯∆(i,j) = n-2, ♯[i]□ = ♯[j]□ = 0, γ_max = 0. Plugging into the formula gives Ric = 2/(n-1) + 2/(n-1) - 2/(n-1) + (n-2)/(n-1) = (2 + 2 - 2 + (n-2))/(n-1) = (n+2)/(n-1)? That does not match n/(n-1). There may be an oversight: perhaps one of the terms in Definition 1 simplifies differently, or the table value is intended for a different normalization, or the formula uses degrees differently. Since the table value is n/(n-1), this suggests either the formula in Definition 1 is mis-copied in the OCR or there is an error in the paper's table. Without the full formula in the excerpt (it cuts off after 'otherwise'), I cannot resolve it definitively, but a careful reader would notice the potential mismatch.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the additive edge selection procedure of SDRF
comments.quote: Calculate vector x where x_{kl} = Ric_{kl}(i,j) - Ric(i,j), the improvement to Ric(i,j) from adding edge k ∼ l where k ∈ B_1(i), l ∈ B_1(j); Sample index k, l with probability softmax(τ x)_{kl} and add edge k ∼ l to G.
comments.explanation: The description of Step 1 is vague. It says 'Calculate vector x where x_{kl} = Ric_{kl}(i,j) - Ric(i,j)'. It is unclear what Ric_{kl}(i,j) denotes. Does it mean the new value of the curvature of edge (i,j) after hypothetically adding edge (k,l)? Or the curvature of the new edge (k,l) itself? The notation is underspecified. The phrase 'the improvement to Ric(i,j) from adding edge k ∼ l' suggests it should be the updated curvature of edge (i,j), but then the subscript 'kl' is confusing. Additionally, the index set for (k,l) is pairs where k ∈ B_1(i) and l ∈ B_1(j), but B_1(i) includes i itself? Typically B_1(i) is the set of nodes within distance 1, i.e., neighbors plus i. If k = i or l = j, adding a self-loop or a double edge might be allowed, but the graph is simple and undirected. The authors should clarify the exact candidates. This ambiguity makes the algorithm partially unreproducible without guessing.

comments.title: Potential misstatement of Proposition 5's lower bound on h_G
comments.quote: If Ric(i,j) ≥ k > 0 for all i ∼ j, then λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2.
comments.explanation: Proposition 5 states that under positive curvature everywhere, the Cheeger constant h_G is at least k/2. However, the summary earlier says 'Proposition 5: If Ric(i,j) ≥ k > 0 for all edges i∼j, then λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2.' Combined with the Cheeger inequality λ_1/2 ≤ h_G, this would imply λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2 and also h_G ≥ λ_1/2, so h_G and λ_1/2 would be equal, which is generally false. There is a chain of inequalities: Cheeger gives λ_1/2 ≤ h_G ≤ √(2λ_1). Proposition 5 appears to claim h_G ≥ k/2, which is a lower bound on h_G. That is fine. But the inequality 'λ_1/2 ≥ h_G' would reverse the Cheeger inequality and seems to be a typo. Likely the authors meant 'λ_1 ≥ k' or 'h_G ≥ k/2' without the reversed inequality. The quote as given includes 'λ_1/2 ≥ h_G', which is contradictory to standard spectral theory. Since this is likely a formatting error in the passage (the authors would not claim λ_1/2 ≥ h_G as that would contradict Cheeger), it needs flagging.

comments.title: Unclear justification for the claim that SDRF 'decreases bottlenecks without significantly increasing connectivity among nodes with different labels'
comments.quote: SDRF modifies edges mainly around the most negatively curved edges, decreasing bottlenecks without significantly increasing connectivity among nodes with different labels, making it more suitable for low-homophily settings where long-range dependencies are more relevant.
comments.explanation: This claim appears in the summary but is not explicitly argued in the conclusion passage. It asserts that the SDRF method does not significantly increase cross-label connectivity. However, the paper's theoretical analysis (Theorems 4 and 6) ties negative curvature to bottlenecks, but does not establish any relationship between curvature and node labels. The homophily measure H(G) is mentioned in the summary but not invoked in the curvature analysis. The claim seems to be an empirical observation or an extrapolation, but is presented in the summary as a key conclusion. In the passage itself, the conclusion only says 'we show one such possibility... and comment on the advantages of surgical method such as this.' No formal justification for the label-agnostic property is given. This is a questionable claim that may overstate what has been shown.

comments.quote: Then there exists Q_j ⊂ S_2(i) satisfying |Q_j| > δ^{-1} and for 0 ≤ ℓ_0 ≤ L-2 we have ...
comments.explanation: The notation S_2(i) appears without a formal definition in the provided excerpt. Earlier in Section 2.1, the paper defines S_r(i) as the sphere of radius r (set of nodes at distance exactly r from i). This is standard, but the reader might momentarily question whether S_2(i) includes i itself or only distance-2 nodes. The context makes it clear that Q_j is a subset of nodes at distance 2 from i, so this is not a major issue, but explicitly restating the definition in Theorem 4 would improve clarity.

comments.title: Condition (ii) in Theorem 4 uses Undefined γ_max(i,j) with no explicit bound
comments.quote: γ_max(i,j) ≤ 1/4 d_i^2
comments.explanation: The quantity γ_max(i,j) is defined earlier as 'the maximal number of 4-cycles based at i∼j traversing a common node'. However, the bound γ_max(i,j) ≤ 1/4 d_i^2 is not derived or justified in the text. While this bound may follow from combinatorial reasoning (e.g., each common node can be involved in at most (d_i choose something) cycles), the authors do not provide a proof or reference. Since this bound is used to ensure that the curvature condition (ii) is well-behaved, a brief justification would strengthen the argument.

comments.title: Overclaim in abstract regarding 'first theoretical study'
comments.quote: This paper, to our knowledge, is the first theoretical study of the bottleneck and over-squashing phenomena in message passing neural networks from a geometric perspective.
comments.explanation: While the paper is indeed a pioneering geometric analysis, the phrase 'first theoretical study' is slightly overbroad. Prior work (Alon & Yahav, 2021) already provided theoretical discussion (though not geometric) of bottlenecks and over-squashing. The authors qualify this with 'from a geometric perspective', which is more accurate, but the phrasing in the abstract could be read as claiming the first theoretical study overall. This is a minor framing issue that does not affect the paper's contributions.

comments.title: Misapplied indexing in Lemma 1's statement: 's ∈ S_{r+1}(i)' unclear
comments.quote: Let i, s ∈ V with s ∈ S_{r+1}(i) . If |∇φ_ℓ| ≤ α and |∇ψ_ℓ| ≤ β for 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ r, then
comments.explanation: The notation S_{r+1}(i) is not defined in the passage. The authors previously defined S_r(i) (the sphere of radius r) but used subscript 1 for one-hop neighbors earlier (S_1(i)), so S_{r+1}(i) is presumably the set of nodes at distance exactly r+1 from i. However, the lemma is supposed to bound the Jacobian ∂h_i^{(r)}/∂x_s, where s is at distance r+1. The use of S_{r+1}(i) is consistent with the context (the example mentions d_G(i,s)=r+1), so it is clear what is intended. This is a minor ambiguity in notation that a careful reader can resolve, but it would be better to formally define S_r(i) earlier in the passage (it is only used implicitly). Not a real error.

comments.title: Missing exponent in Lemma 1: bound likely involves (Â)^{r+1} but text shows Â^{r+1}
comments.explanation: The OCR has introduced formatting errors: the exponent in '(Aˆ [r] +1)' should be read as (Â)^{r+1} (the (r+1)-th power of the normalized augmented adjacency matrix). Similarly '2 − 1 3 − r' is meant to be '2^{-1} 3^{-r}'. The example assumes a binary tree where each non-root node has degree 3 inside the receptive field, leading to (Â)^{r+1}_{is} = (1/2) * (1/3)^r. The intended meaning is clear from the context and standard GNN analysis. No real issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent index in Theorem 4: L is depth, but condition uses ℓ_0 ≤ L-2
comments.quote: Consider a MPNN as in equation 1. Let i ∼ j with d_i ≤ d_j and assume that: (i) |∇φ_ℓ| ≤ α and |∇ψ_ℓ| ≤ β for each 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ L − 1, with L ≥ 2 the depth of the MPNN. Then there exists Q_j ⊂ S_2(i) satisfying |Q_j| > δ^{−1} and for 0 ≤ ℓ_0 ≤ L − 2 we have
comments.explanation: The theorem states L ≥ 2, but then requires ℓ_0 ≤ L-2. For L=2, this forces ℓ_0=0 only, which is fine. However, the result is supposed to show that negatively curved edges cause over-squashing over two layers from node i to nodes at distance 2 (since Q_j ⊂ S_2(i)). The bound involves the Jacobian computed at layer ℓ_0+2. The condition ℓ_0 ≤ L-2 ensures that the MPNN has at least ℓ_0+2 layers to propagate. This is logically consistent: for L=2, ℓ_0=0 is the only allowed value. The text could be clearer about why the bound depends on the depth, but it is not an error.

comments.title: Potential sign error in Proposition 5: inequality direction for Cheeger constant
comments.quote: If Ric(i, j) ≥ k > 0 for all i ∼ j, then λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2.
comments.explanation: The Cheeger inequality states λ_1/2 ≤ h_G ≤ √(2λ_1) (for the normalized Laplacian). The statement here says λ_1/2 ≥ h_G, which would be the reverse of the lower bound. However, the authors reference Lin et al. (2011) and Theorem 2 to derive this. The claim is that positive curvature gives a lower bound on the spectral gap (λ_1), and then via Cheeger inequality, h_G is bounded below by k/2. The inequality direction as written 'λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2' would imply λ_1 ≥ 2h_G and h_G ≥ k/2. This is plausible if λ_1 is large enough. But the standard Cheeger inequality for the normalized Laplacian is h_G ≥ λ_1/2 (the 'easy' direction). So the statement λ_1/2 ≥ h_G is the opposite of the usual inequality. Unless the paper uses a different normalization or defines λ_1 differently (e.g., the largest eigenvalue of some other matrix), this appears to be a sign error. Given that the next section discusses diffusion-based rewiring and Cheeger constant, this could mislead a reader about the relationship between curvature and bottleneck. This is a moderate issue because it may indicate a misunderstanding of the Cheeger inequality direction or a nonstandard convention.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of softmax sampling in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Sample index k, l with probability softmax(τ x)_kl and add edge k ∼ l to G.
comments.explanation: The description says 'Calculate vector x where x_{kl} = Ric_{kl}(i,j) − Ric(i,j), the improvement to Ric(i,j) from adding edge k ∼ l where k ∈ B_1(i), l ∈ B_1(j)'. It then says 'Sample index k, l with probability softmax(τ x)_kl'. The softmax function is typically applied to a vector, but x here is described as having entries indexed by pairs (k,l). It is unclear whether x is flattened into a vector or if a 2D softmax (over both indices) is intended. Additionally, 'Ric_{kl}(i,j)' is not defined; the notation suggests it might be the curvature of edge (i,j) after adding edge (k,l), but this is not explained. The algorithm is underspecified: a reader cannot reproduce how to compute the improvement x_{kl} without knowing how Recomputing curvature after each edge addition is handled. This makes the method difficult to implement as described.

comments.title: Contradiction in curvature sign convention for tree example
comments.quote: In a discrete hyperbolic geometry (Figure 2c), the mutual distance of the edge endpoints would have grown compared to that of i and j (tree).
comments.explanation: This prose correctly associates trees with negative curvature (hyperbolic geometry). However, earlier Figure 2 caption labels (c) as 'Tree (<0)', which is consistent. The confusion arises later when discussing Figure 3 and the calculation of ♯_□: the authors say 'both without 4,6 because of the triangle 1-6-0'. This is fine. The potential issue is that the curvature definition (Definition 1) is not fully displayed; only a partial formula is shown. The passage says 'The curvature is negative when i ∼ j behaves as a bridge between S_1(i) and S_1(j)'. This is consistent with the tree example. No actual error here, but the partial formula for Ric(i,j) in the OCR (with missing terms) makes it impossible to verify the curvature values in Table 1. For instance, Table 1 says for a tree T_r, Ric = 4/(r+1) − 2, which is negative for r ≥ 2. The reader cannot verify this from the truncated formula. This is more of an OCR truncation issue than an author error, but it affects understanding.

comments.title: Incorrect inequality in Proposition 5 (Cheeger constant bound)
comments.quote: **Proposition 5.** _If_ Ric( _i, j_ ) _≥ k >_ 0 _for any edge i ∼ j, then λ_ 1 _/_ 2 _≥ hG ≥[k]_ 2 _._
comments.explanation: The Cheeger inequality for the normalized Laplacian (Chung & Graham, 1997) states λ₁ ≤ 2h_G, i.e., h_G ≥ λ₁/2. The proposition claims the opposite inequality λ₁/2 ≥ h_G, which is generally false. For example, a complete graph K_n with large n has λ₁ = n/(n-1) ≈ 1 and h_G ≈ 1 (since the minimum cut is about half the edges), giving λ₁/2 ≈ 0.5 < 1 = h_G. This contradicts the stated bound. The intended inequality is likely h_G ≥ λ₁/2, which together with h_G ≥ k/2 would be correct under the given curvature condition. The reversal appears to be a sign error. This affects the paper's claim about controlling the spectral gap via curvature, and could mislead readers about the relation between curvature and bottleneckedness.

comments.title: Inconsistency in claim about SDRF adding/removing similar numbers of edges
comments.quote: In comparison, SDRF adds and removes a similar number of edges and approximately preserves the degree distribution.
comments.explanation: Table 3 shows that for several datasets, the percentage of edges removed by SDRF is substantially larger than the percentage added. For example, Cornell: +7.8% vs -33.3%; Texas: +2.4% vs -10.4%; Wisconsin: +1.4% vs -7.5%; Actor: +5.4% vs -9.9%. Only about half the datasets (Chameleon, Squirrel, Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) have roughly equal percentages. The statement that SDRF 'adds and removes a similar number of edges' is not universally true and could mislead a reader about the extent of edge removals relative to additions.

comments.title: Theorem 4 conclusion does not match the bound derived in the statement
comments.quote: Then there exists Qj ⊂ S_2(i) satisfying |Qj| > δ^{-1} and for 0 ≤ ℓ_0 ≤ L − 2 we have ... the Jacobian of h_k^{(ℓ_0+2)} w.r.t. x_i is bounded (over-squashing occurs). Negatively curved edges cause bottlenecks.
comments.explanation: The passage states that Theorem 4 proves that 'negatively curved edges are those causing the graph bottleneck and thus leading to the over-squashing phenomenon.' However, the theorem's conclusion as presented appears to claim both that a large set Q_j exists and that the Jacobian is bounded. The notion of 'bounded' here is unclear in context: if the Jacobian is bounded for all nodes in Q_j, that would mean the influence is limited, which is the over-squashing claim. But the inequality involves Ric-like quantities multiplied by powers of the normalized adjacency matrix. Without seeing the full expression in the paper (only the first line is provided in the quote), it is ambiguous whether the bound is a small number (confirming over-squashing) or a trivial bound. The summary says 'the Jacobian ... is bounded (over-squashing occurs)', but a bounded Jacobian could be either large or small. The reader cannot verify that the bound indeed implies a non-trivial restriction of information flow. The authors may have intended to show that the Jacobian is bounded from above by a quantity that is small due to negative curvature, but the excerpt alone does not make this clear.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for Balanced Forman curvature in Definition 1 vs. table
comments.quote: We refer to Ric as Balanced Forman curvature. ... Table 1: Examples of the Balanced Forman curvature. ... Complete K_n: n/(n-1)
comments.explanation: Definition 1 defines the curvature as Ric(i,j), and the prose says 'We refer to Ric as Balanced Forman curvature'. However, in Table 1, the row for the complete graph K_n shows a value of n/(n-1). According to Definition 1, for the complete graph K_n, each edge i∼j has d_i = d_j = n-1, ♯∆(i,j) = n-2, ♯[i]□ = ♯[j]□ = 0, γ_max = 0. Plugging into the formula gives Ric = 2/(n-1) + 2/(n-1) - 2/(n-1) + (n-2)/(n-1) = (2 + 2 - 2 + (n-2))/(n-1) = (n+2)/(n-1)? That does not match n/(n-1). There may be an oversight: perhaps one of the terms in Definition 1 simplifies differently, or the table value is intended for a different normalization, or the formula uses degrees differently. Since the table value is n/(n-1), this suggests either the formula in Definition 1 is mis-copied in the OCR or there is an error in the paper's table. Without the full formula in the excerpt (it cuts off after 'otherwise'), I cannot resolve it definitively, but a careful reader would notice the potential mismatch.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the additive edge selection procedure of SDRF
comments.quote: Calculate vector x where x_{kl} = Ric_{kl}(i,j) - Ric(i,j), the improvement to Ric(i,j) from adding edge k ∼ l where k ∈ B_1(i), l ∈ B_1(j); Sample index k, l with probability softmax(τ x)_{kl} and add edge k ∼ l to G.
comments.explanation: The description of Step 1 is vague. It says 'Calculate vector x where x_{kl} = Ric_{kl}(i,j) - Ric(i,j)'. It is unclear what Ric_{kl}(i,j) denotes. Does it mean the new value of the curvature of edge (i,j) after hypothetically adding edge (k,l)? Or the curvature of the new edge (k,l) itself? The notation is underspecified. The phrase 'the improvement to Ric(i,j) from adding edge k ∼ l' suggests it should be the updated curvature of edge (i,j), but then the subscript 'kl' is confusing. Additionally, the index set for (k,l) is pairs where k ∈ B_1(i) and l ∈ B_1(j), but B_1(i) includes i itself? Typically B_1(i) is the set of nodes within distance 1, i.e., neighbors plus i. If k = i or l = j, adding a self-loop or a double edge might be allowed, but the graph is simple and undirected. The authors should clarify the exact candidates. This ambiguity makes the algorithm partially unreproducible without guessing.

comments.title: Potential misstatement of Proposition 5's lower bound on h_G
comments.quote: If Ric(i,j) ≥ k > 0 for all i ∼ j, then λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2.
comments.explanation: Proposition 5 states that under positive curvature everywhere, the Cheeger constant h_G is at least k/2. However, the summary earlier says 'Proposition 5: If Ric(i,j) ≥ k > 0 for all edges i∼j, then λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2.' Combined with the Cheeger inequality λ_1/2 ≤ h_G, this would imply λ_1/2 ≥ h_G ≥ k/2 and also h_G ≥ λ_1/2, so h_G and λ_1/2 would be equal, which is generally false. There is a chain of inequalities: Cheeger gives λ_1/2 ≤ h_G ≤ √(2λ_1). Proposition 5 appears to claim h_G ≥ k/2, which is a lower bound on h_G. That is fine. But the inequality 'λ_1/2 ≥ h_G' would reverse the Cheeger inequality and seems to be a typo. Likely the authors meant 'λ_1 ≥ k' or 'h_G ≥ k/2' without the reversed inequality. The quote as given includes 'λ_1/2 ≥ h_G', which is contradictory to standard spectral theory. Since this is likely a formatting error in the passage (the authors would not claim λ_1/2 ≥ h_G as that would contradict Cheeger), it needs flagging.

comments.title: Unclear justification for the claim that SDRF 'decreases bottlenecks without significantly increasing connectivity among nodes with different labels'
comments.quote: SDRF modifies edges mainly around the most negatively curved edges, decreasing bottlenecks without significantly increasing connectivity among nodes with different labels, making it more suitable for low-homophily settings where long-range dependencies are more relevant.
comments.explanation: This claim appears in the summary but is not explicitly argued in the conclusion passage. It asserts that the SDRF method does not significantly increase cross-label connectivity. However, the paper's theoretical analysis (Theorems 4 and 6) ties negative curvature to bottlenecks, but does not establish any relationship between curvature and node labels. The homophily measure H(G) is mentioned in the summary but not invoked in the curvature analysis. The claim seems to be an empirical observation or an extrapolation, but is presented in the summary as a key conclusion. In the passage itself, the conclusion only says 'we show one such possibility... and comment on the advantages of surgical method such as this.' No formal justification for the label-agnostic property is given. This is a questionable claim that may overstate what has been shown.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This is a rigorous and well-motivated theoretical contribution addressing a critical limitation in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs): the over-squashing of messages. The paper establishes a clear theoretical connection between information propagation delays and graph topology by introducing a novel 'Balanced Forman curvature' metric that provides a sharp lower bound to existing curvature measures. The writing style is formal and accessible, effectively utilizing standard geometric deep learning notations to guide the reader through the motivation and formal definition of the problem. The most significant issue based on the provided text, however, is the reliance on upcoming sections to substantiate the manuscript’s impact; specifically, the paper must convincingly demonstrate that the identified negative curvature edges are indeed responsible for bottlenecks and that the proposed Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow rewiring method effectively alleviates over-squashing in complex graph structures.

comments.title: Cheeger Inequality Direction
comments.quote: \lambda_1 _/_ 2 _\ge hG \ge[k]_ 2 _[.]_
comments.explanation: The text states the Cheeger inequality as \lambda_1/2 \ge h_G, implying h_G \le \lambda_1/2. The standard Cheeger inequality for graphs (using the first non-zero eigenvalue as the spectral gap) asserts the reverse: h_G \ge \lambda_1/2 (or \lambda_1 \le 2h). Stating the bound in the reverse direction is a fundamental logical error that undermines the subsequent discussion relating curvature to the spectral gap.

comments.title: Sentence Fragment in Definition
comments.quote: Given i \in V, we denote its degree by di and let [where dG is the standard shortest-path distance on the graph and r \in N.]
comments.explanation: The sentence structure is syntactically broken. It begins with 'let' but lacks the subject defining the object of the 'let' (likely B_r(i)). It then immediately follows with 'where dG is...'. This makes the definition of the receptive field B_r(i) unclear and difficult to parse in isolation.

comments.title: Misleading Description of Normalization
comments.quote: Here we have used the augmented normalized adjacency matrix to propagate messages from each node to its neighbors, which simply leads to a degree normalization of the message functions \psi_\ell.
comments.explanation: The text claims the adjacency matrix leads to a 'degree normalization of the message functions.' Strictly speaking, the normalized adjacency matrix performs degree normalization on the *aggregation* (the sum over neighbors), whereas the 'message functions' typically map two representations to a new representation. The phrasing is slightly loose and could mislead a reader about the exact location (aggregation vs. function argument) of the normalization step.

comments.title: Proposition 5 - Garbled Inequality for Cheeger Constant
comments.explanation: The LaTeX equation in the proposition is corrupted by OCR, appearing as `λ_1 / 2 ≥ h_G ≥ [k]^2`. The notation `[k]` is ambiguous and likely represents a fraction like `k/2` or `k^2/2` rather than a bracket around the variable `k`. The chain `λ_1 / 2 ≥ h_G` is also unusual, as the standard Cheeger inequality usually relates `h_G` to `sqrt(λ_1)` (e.g., `h_G ≤ sqrt(2λ_1)`). Without the original text, it is impossible to verify the claimed relationship between the Ricci curvature lower bound `k` and the Cheeger constant `h_G`. While this is likely an extraction artifact, it blocks the verification of a key theoretical claim.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 - Ambiguous Notation for Edge Improvement Calculation
comments.quote: Calculate vector **x** where xkl = Ric_kl(i, j) − Ric(i, j), the improvement to Ric(i, j) from adding edge k ∼ l where k ∈ B1(i), l ∈ B1(j);
comments.explanation: The notation `Ric_kl(i, j)` is confusing. The text clarifies this is the 'improvement to Ric(i, j)' (i.e., the new curvature value of edge `(i, j)` after adding edge `(k, l)`). However, the subscript `kl` combined with the arguments `(i, j)` makes it unclear whether `(k, l)` defines a new curvature type or a derived property of edge `(i, j)`. Standard pseudocode would likely use notation like `Ric(i, j; + (k, l))` or explicitly state `ΔRic(i, j)` to avoid ambiguity with the definition of standard curvature `Ric(i, j)`.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 - Underspecified Stopping Condition
comments.quote: Remove edge ... if Ric(i, j) > C_+. Until convergence, or max iterations reached;
comments.explanation: The algorithm describes a step to remove an edge only if its curvature exceeds a threshold $C_+$. It does not specify what happens if no edge in the graph satisfies $Ric(i, j) > C_+$ (e.g., if the graph is already dense and positively curved). In this case, the 'add' step would have no target (no most negative edge), or if a target exists (negative edge), the algorithm would endlessly add edges without removing any, potentially inflating the graph size indefinitely. The method lacks a clause to terminate when the removal condition cannot be met.

comments.title: Reference formatting - Missing comma in author names
comments.quote: Fan RK Chung Graham. Spectral graph theory. Number 92. American Mathematical Soc., 1997.
comments.explanation: The text reads 'Fan RK Chung Graham' which is inconsistent with standard citation conventions (e.g., 'Fan, Chung Graham' or 'Fan Chung Graham'). This appears to be an OCR artifact or a formatting error where punctuation was lost between the initials or names.

comments.title: Reference formatting - Missing comma in author names
comments.quote: Linton C Freeman. A set of measures of centrality based on betweenness.
comments.explanation: Similar to the above, the entry 'Linton C Freeman' lacks the comma separating the first name initials from the surname ('Linton C. Freeman' or 'Linton Freeman'), which is standard for author lists.

comments.title: Reference formatting - OCR artifact in author name
comments.quote: Ronald R Coifman and Stéphane Lafon. Diffusion maps. Applied and computational harmonic analysis, 21(1):5-30, 2006.
comments.explanation: The citation includes 'Ronald R Coifman'. While Ronald R. Coifman exists in the literature, the standard citation is typically 'Ronald Coifman'. The inclusion of 'R' seems unnecessary and may be an OCR misread of a period (e.g., 'Ronald R. Coifman') or a typo.

comments.title: Reference formatting - Missing article 'The'
comments.quote: Applied and computational harmonic analysis, 21(1):5-30, 2006.
comments.explanation: Journal names are conventionally capitalized and include the definite article 'The'. The text is missing 'The' at the beginning of the journal name.
